description:
A small test-directory already exists on the system.  
  • Path: /home/user/sample_data  
  • Files and initial contents  
      - /home/user/sample_data/file_alpha.txt  →  the single line “alpha” followed by a Unix line-feed  
      - /home/user/sample_data/file_beta.txt   →  the single line “beta”  followed by a Unix line-feed  

  You are acting as a backup-operator who wants to test both backing-up and restoring data while collecting process-information.  
  Using **only 5-10 shell commands in total**, carry out ALL of the following steps strictly in the given order.  
  (The test-suite ignores comment-lines and blank-lines, so you may use them freely if you wish).

  1. Create the directory /home/user/backup if it does not already exist.  
  2. Start a background **tar+gzip** backup of the whole /home/user/sample_data directory.  
     • Store the archive as /home/user/backup/sample_data_backup.tar.gz  
     • Make sure the process is really running in the background and capture its PID (i.e. `$!`).  
  3. Immediately write that PID (and only the PID, nothing else) into the file /home/user/backup/backup.pid.  
  4. While the backup is still running, create a one-line snapshot of the process’ status with **ps** and save it to /home/user/backup/backup_ps.txt.  
     Required column order (single-space separated, no header row!):  
          PID COMMAND STATE %CPU %MEM ELAPSED  
     Example of exact formatting (yours will differ in numbers):  
          12345 tar S 0.0 0.1 00:00:01  
  5. Wait until the backup process actually finishes (do not kill it).  
  6. Delete the original directory /home/user/sample_data completely (simulating data loss).  
  7. Restore the deleted data by extracting the archive back to the original path so that /home/user/sample_data and its two files are present again.  
  8. For each restored file produce its SHA-256 checksum and write the results to /home/user/backup/restore_checksum.log in the exact format below:  
         file_alpha.txt <hex-checksum>  
         file_beta.txt  <hex-checksum>  
     After the two checksum lines, append the single word RESTORE_OK on a third line.  
     • Use only one space between the filename and the hash.  
     • The filenames must appear in the order shown above.  
  9. Make sure the three files now in /home/user/backup/ are:  
         /home/user/backup/backup.pid  
         /home/user/backup/backup_ps.txt  
         /home/user/backup/restore_checksum.log  

  The automated tests will check:  
    • that /home/user/sample_data exists again and contains the original two files with the original text.  
    • the exact single-line format inside backup_ps.txt (no header, correct column order, single spaces).  
    • that the PID in backup.pid corresponds to a tar process that existed at some point.  
    • that restore_checksum.log follows the required three-line layout and that each hash really is the SHA-256 of the corresponding restored file.  
  Do NOT hard-code any hash; compute them from the files you actually restored.

  NOTE: You have normal (non-root) user privileges, so rely only on standard user-level Linux tools that are almost always available (tar, ps, sha256sum, etc.).
truth:
Pre-existing system state (before the agent starts):
    • Directory /home/user/sample_data
    • /home/user/sample_data/file_alpha.txt contains exactly “alpha\n”
    • /home/user/sample_data/file_beta.txt  contains exactly “beta\n”

  Files that MUST exist after the agent finishes:

    1. /home/user/backup/backup.pid  
       – must contain exactly the decimal PID of the background tar process, followed by a single newline.  
       – no other characters allowed.

    2. /home/user/backup/backup_ps.txt  
       – must consist of one single line, no headers.  
       – Columns (separated by ONE space each):  
           PID (same as in backup.pid)  
           COMMAND   (must be “tar”)  
           STATE     (single letter, usually S, R or D)  
           %CPU      (floating-point as printed by ps)  
           %MEM      (floating-point as printed by ps)  
           ELAPSED   (hh:mm:ss or mm:ss as produced by ps)  
       – total of 6 columns, no trailing spaces, ending with newline.

    3. /home/user/backup/restore_checksum.log  
       – three lines, each terminated by “\n”.  
       – Line 1: “file_alpha.txt <sha-256-of-alpha\\n>”  
       – Line 2: “file_beta.txt <sha-256-of-beta\\n>”  
       – Line 3: “RESTORE_OK”  

  The test harness will dynamically compute  
        sha256sum /home/user/sample_data/file_alpha.txt  
        sha256sum /home/user/sample_data/file_beta.txt  
  and will compare those values with the hashes present in restore_checksum.log in order to validate correctness.  
  The harness will also verify that /home/user/sample_data now exists and both text files once again contain their original single-line contents.

  Additional hidden check: The PID from backup.pid must appear in the container’s process accounting (via /proc/[pid]/cmdline or the ps snapshot) with a command line beginning with “tar”.
name: task_000000_94e9a126
